segments.text: meeting open.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Welcome everybody.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So we'll start with the apologies, and I guess we have Hazel, everyone else seems to be here I think.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So could I have a mover please?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Andrew, just before you do, I have to leave the meeting at 1.30 to attend a future proof meeting, so sorry about that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So my apologies from then.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Good as gold, thanks Jim.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So we include that in the
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: in the motion as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: If someone would like to move, please.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thanks, Lou.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thanks, Susan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All in favour?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Aye.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Contrary no, it's carried.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Disclosure of members' interests.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Do we have any today?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No, I'll move on to item four, late items.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Any late items?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No late items.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So confirmation of order of meeting.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No changes, Ken?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No, I don't see any need for change.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: We do have a number of guests joining us at the meeting today, but they're well aware of the time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So yes, I'm hoping they'll be here at the required time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Happy to move, Andrew.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Graham.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thanks, Graham.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: A seconder.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Second, Susan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thanks, Susan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I think that was...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All in favour please say aye.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Aye.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Country no carried.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Okay, item six, which is confirmation of the minutes from 18 February.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Have we all, I'll take those as being read.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Anything raised from those minutes?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Andrew Graham speaking.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: My diary showing me that I was at their meeting, but my name's not recorded yet.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_04

segments.text: Right.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Yeah, I was going to bring that up too.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: So yeah, thank you some mistakes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_03

segments.text: Thanks, Bruce.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: OK, so I'm sure we can have that remedy, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yes, we'll make the changes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: OK, so with that change, could I have a mover for the minutes to be received as a trim correct record, please?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thank you, Marcus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Seconder.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I'll second it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thanks, Bruce.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All in favour, please say aye.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Aye.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No carried.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Item seven, Jason.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Has Jason joined us?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: He has.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I see him nodding.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Hi, Jason.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: How are you doing?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Good.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thank you.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Great.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And the floor is yours.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Sam, I don't think we had issues with trying to get the presentation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Is that right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Oh, no.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Sam is sharing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Brilliant.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Perfect.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Good morning.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Well, good afternoon.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Sorry, everyone.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks for allowing me time just to give you a quick update on where we are as a sector for tourism and particularly for Waipa District.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So a report has obviously been provided to you and prepared, which was our last six months performance at the end of 31st December, 2019.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: As you would have seen, Waipa District Council was going through significant growth, probably one of the fastest growing
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: regions for us with regards to tourism impact.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You were experiencing double digit growth pretty much through visitor expenditure and also commercial accommodation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But we are here today to discuss just obviously a bit of a change in the dynamic given COVID and the impact for tourism, particularly as a sector.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Go to the next slide, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Perfect.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So just first, a couple of slides, obviously, just covering off some data.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We're now receiving international and national data on a weekly basis from the government and also from the airlines.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So no surprise, obviously, since COVID lockdown and international travel restrictions were put in place.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: International visitor arrivals into New Zealand have been dropping at a very sharp rate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We still do have airline connectivity, which I will go through
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: because we are still maintaining what we call a baseline service for connections across the world, mainly for repatriation flights, but also for cargo and service across the country as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Next slide, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So weekly card transactions, again, no surprise.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Obviously, overall, our visitor expenditure is down significantly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But when you do look onto the left top five, obviously, food, liquor, and pharmacies, we are still seeing some cumulative and some, obviously, weekly growth in expenditure compared to all our other visitor type expenditure and transactions as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So the impact for us as a sector, again, look, I mean, all of this has been reported, but just to cover off in summary, we were already impacted through the loss of China Group travel, which started in the second to last week of January.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So with that, all of our international-facing products, our hotels, our motels, our Airbnb's were immediately impacted by that because many of China Group travel actually did stay in the Waikato.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: They were our fourth largest international visitor market.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: behind Australia, North America and UK and Europe.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: It did shut us down, pretty much once international travel restrictions hit us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And the visitor economy covers more than just the tourism operators.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Again, we've mentioned accommodation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: It also impacts retail, hospitality, transport providers, our operators, of course, but also event organisers and major venues.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And of course, Mystery Creek of Antitrome and Lake Harapetal are key event platforms for Waiipa district.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We have had significant job losses and business closures across tourism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I have down underneath that last bullet point, look the immediate business impact that we have been coaching our businesses through and helping them get through.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: As some have gone to what we call temporary or seasonal closure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, we have a lot of seasonality and tourism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: many who would close for winter have closed but those that potentially have never closed before we've suggested they go into hibernation and so that's what we're calling temporary seasonal closures.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We've had a number of businesses you know Hobbiton was obviously one of the most public ones reported in the media but again all our motels hotels retail hospitality
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: have pretty much reduced service and staff, there's only essential services operating as we know, but again with alert level three we do see some opening up potentially in the hospitality sector for wire power anyway.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And then the last obviously implication for us is we have had a number of businesses who are permanently closing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Not only are these are lost definitely to the tourism industry to try and bring people back when we don't have products,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: but also particularly for those vulnerable communities where some of these operators have been going for many years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Can I ask a question about that?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Sure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Have you got examples of what businesses are struggling and who is closing and how it is impacting around the white industry?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yeah, so particularly for WIPAR, it has mainly been your hospitality, potentially some of your retail, and also accommodation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we've had many, nearly all of your accommodation has pretty much gone into hibernation or closure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: They're calling it temporary closure, but some of them have been able to access the wage subsidy, which is great.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But again, what we're gonna see is the impacts are longer than the 12 week wage subsidy right now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So it's post 12 weeks is what we're quite concerned about.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We have been lobbying nationally the government and I think that's all sectors not just tourism around potentially extending the wage subsidy for 20 weeks because it will take a lot to restart the tourism sector again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: People's propensity to travel is quite low.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We will travel locally and potentially we will travel intra-regionally, sustain within the region, but to try and stand up the domestic travel market is going to be a long haul that we're looking at a six to 12 months recovery plan for that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Great.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So, lastly, I mean, no surprise here, this was just something we do for industries, people know where we all fit.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So from the tourism perspective when we respond, obviously we're providing industry support right now at a national level, regional level and also
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: at a local level, but then we have Tuwaka, our Chambers of Commerce, our business associations, and all local government have been great in actually supporting us with what we call the business and community support side for tourism businesses.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And then lastly, we're obviously partnered with the Civil Defence Network around providing welfare support, not just for tourism operators.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Again, it's also been utilising tourism operators.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Many of our caterers have now been repurposed into providing
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: food services pretty much or supporting the food banks and actually cooking meals.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We also have a number of our transport operators and tourism being basically requisitions now to provide transport options and actually getting people around such as essential workers and many of the staff potentially we've been redeploying into the civil defence network through providing security and welfare and call centre as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So not only are we also working with civil defence we're also putting staff into there as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Even though we're obviously dealing with COVID, is it just for you to be aware there are obviously continuing issues that were going on with...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: with COVID and with the tourism industry.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: First up was domestic airline connectivity.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So look, Air New Zealand has been pretty open that they will not be restarting until alert level one.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we meet with Air New Zealand weekly just to see how we will try and stand the industry back up again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But they're going to focus very much on what we call an incremental restart.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So that's doing a width and breadth strategy across the country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So potentially maybe only two or three flights into Hamilton.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But again, it's to try and get that reach across the whole network first before they can actually stand up and have a full schedule like we have seen at a Hamilton Airport, particularly over the last three to four years.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Airline connectivity internationally is still continuing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So those are just a summary of some of the ones that are still flying.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So looking at New Zealand are still connecting into North America and Hong Kong.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: into Australia, Fiji Airlines are still flying into Fiji on a daily service and Qatar has probably used the most to try and repatriate Northern Hemisphere travelers back to their homes through Qatar and then also Korean Airlines are still operating a daily service as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: For future again, Air New Zealand is saying they will be focusing very much on the domestic market first.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: They will traditionally be a domestic airline for the first six to 12 months and domestic also includes Australia and the Pacific.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: American Airlines so far have been the only one we've met with who plan to return to New Zealand from October 2020.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So they have put a stake in the ground to say when they will start flying into New Zealand again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Now the reason why I just raised international airline connectivity is because it will take this country at least two to five years before we ever go back to the type of connectivity we've had as a country previously before lockdown.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, it's going to be a very long burn for us as a country to regain international visitor arrivals we've seen in the past.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Air traffic control services are up for review.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: The reason why I just put that in there is just to let everyone know there was no Waikato airports or airfields, but particularly Hamilton Airport isn't impacted.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But for us in the central North Island, Rotorua is one that is going to be impacted around potentially the closure of air traffic control tower services.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: That will mean potentially that we will be re-dividing the domestic network through Turanga and Hamilton.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So unfortunately for Rotorua's last, it could be our gain.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And then lastly, we've still got the investigation underway around Fakati, White Island.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So the WorkSafe investigation should be coming to a close shortly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: There will be impacts on the tourism and suppliers around adventure tourism for this country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And also we will be upweighting Qualmark, which is our accreditation program, particularly around health and safety for the country.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Just some event updates, we're now, Field Days is going online.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So for the 30th of June for two weeks, Field Days will be providing a virtual exhibition space, very similar to other international events who offer this type of experience.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Again, it is a trial, but we do hope to bring Field Days back in 2021.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Obviously not only is it the largest major event for Waipara, but it's definitely for the Waikato with regards to economic impact.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We worked due to host the New Zealand Tourism Awards in November that has been postponed until next year, so we will reset the date for that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Exploring trends, these are what we call our two main international trade shows.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: They've obviously been delayed, Trends 2021, which is the main time when we actually sell the Waikato to the world.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We will not be having until maybe June next year, so we're still waiting on a date for that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Two major convention centers were due to open this year, Te Pai and Christchurch, which potentially is the one that will be taking a lot of business away for us for business events.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: That has now been delayed for another year.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And of course, the New Zealand International Convention Center, we will not know the reopening date of that until they work through the final insurance claims.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, by having these two convention centers not operating,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: means that places like Croadlands and the Mystery Creek Event Centre are going to be filling the gap for national and international conferences for the short term, which is actually a good opportunity for us again as a region post COVID.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But we also do still have events planned.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So the major events, obviously the National Waka Ama is at this stage still planned to take effect in Lake Karapero and we also have our first major event for the region, the ICC Women's Cricket World Cup scheduled for the 8th of February again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: This one will particularly be
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: decided by around October November whether we international travel restrictions have lifted to enable some of the players to come.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Otherwise they will have to go into if it does continue into isolation until the cricket World Cup can be held.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: What we have seen particularly for other parts of the sector is that events have been more postponed for Waipa and for the wider Waikato region rather than completely cancelled and not returning.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So there is some positivity for us as we do see this as the lead sector to inter-recovery economically and socially for the district.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Next slide, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We have a three tiered response plan.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Obviously this is no similar to everyone else right now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We have a mitigate process which we're currently in now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We then have a restart phase and then we have a reimagine phase.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So initially in the response phase, no different to everyone else.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Lots of briefings.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: A lot of support, we're obviously having face-to-face briefings across all the sector long before the shutdown.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And now we've moved to the virtual space.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We're still doing advocacy at a national level to try and make sure we can access more business support for the region.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Some of the gap right now out of government support and out of the support that Tawaka can provide is it doesn't cover any employees for 100 employees plus.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So that's quite the pointy end of town, we call it, for most of our region, for our big employers.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Again, wage subsidy, okay, but outside of that, there's actually no large industry support.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we've been pushing that through government to try and unlock some of that if this lockdown continues.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We've been doing one-on-one operator check-ins.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We have 240 listed operators with us.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, these are daily to weekly check-ins by all our teams to make sure everyone's been able to access business advice and some of the wage support.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We've been doing webinars and we've now moved in the last two weeks into workforce redeployment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, we've been taking some organisations or employers that have maybe at least five to 10 staff members displaced through to Hobbiton who had one of the largest that around 270 and actually redeploying them and offering them redeployment options into the Kiwi Fruit industry.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We placed 110 staff into Kiwi Fruit with transport from Hobbiton.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: as well as the avocado industry, which will be the next seasonal workforce will be will be standing up until the end of the year.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We've put staff into supermarkets, civil defence, security, and also they've been providing some services from home.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: But again, what we are going to see over the next two weeks, and we're starting to measure that is the full impacts of people that we cannot redeploy that potentially will be out of work.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So there's currently a survey underway and we'll be updating all councils and the government around the overall impact for tourism of Waikato.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Under the restart phase, so again, we want to congratulate local government because obviously getting some of those shovel-roody projects through with the Crown infrastructure partners for their consideration is a great win for the region, but potentially for the sector.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Pretty much every district has put up around one to two tourism
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: projects as part of Shovel Ready.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, we do need to focus on the future and how we will restart the region.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Research, we were already in market and about to finish up our domestic perceptions and major research.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: This is now becoming quite timely for us and will help us inform around how we will try and bring back the domestic travellers into the region based on some of the research we've undertaken.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We're also working on a major and business events restart campaign, so that planning has been underway for a couple of weeks, so that when we're ready to go, we can push go on that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We do see events and business events and corporate travel as being the kickstarter we need for economic and social recovery across the region.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: New developments, again, you've all got new developments going on potentially in your regions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: in Lake and obviously one of your newest hotels in Cambridge only opened on Christmas Eve so Glenda and the team we've been keeping in touch with quite a lot because they haven't been in operation very long.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Their impacts are quite severe so again making sure that we still have some future developments that will continue across the region to take us through the next stage.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Data and insights
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I've touched on they've now gone to weekly so again we're sharing that information in Tuwaka and now we're contributing to the Tuwaka economic outlook as well which is great and then lastly the mighty local campaign was launched two weeks ago and that's definitely around the restart phase.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So mighty local again as a partnership with ourselves to Walker all the chambers of commerce so to our mood to and obviously Cambridge Chamber of Commerce, plus the Waikato Chamber of Commerce all our councils, the business associations that also exist in some communities and all our regional sites.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We're in phase one.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So for the last two weeks, we've been promoting businesses with contactless delivery, promoting, you know, people that are still doing entertainment online or virtually from within Waikato, recipes from our beloved chefs, wellbeing and fitness.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And this week we launched business stories.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, we're starting to use this platform to promote some of the businesses and how they're pivoting into obviously response or looking at different opportunities for their business.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So the idea is to try and give people hope during phase one.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: During phase two, which is obviously from alert level three, we will increase our takeaway and Ethan B listings and also essential services are starting to grow as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: In phase two, we will move more to a local phase at the moment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: It's a regional platform, but the idea is potentially people will be able to group the types of information and services available at a local community level and at a district level as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And then phase three, which has been hopefully all travel restrictions are lifted.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We will then activate the basically the buy local, eat local, event local shop, visit and explore within the region.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And obviously that's in partnership with many communities and business associations that already have local programs such as Cambridge with their totally locally Cambridge campaign.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we're working with Kelly and the team around how we try and push their program rather than this one once we get to phase three.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Thanks, Sam.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And when it comes to how we will actually respond long term, so at the moment we're in phase one, so we're calling it the hyper-local phase.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So one to three months, we'll be pushing basically localism, everything to do with obviously how you purchase and interact with your local community.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: At phase two, which then we'll move into a three to six month bracket, we're estimating we can then start to do intra-regional travel,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we're targeting the drive market within the Waikato region.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We're hopefully starting to stand up community events again that can happen at a local level and regional business meetings might be able to begin again with social distancing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: At level three, which is what we're planning our scenarios for six to 12 months, we can then stand up the domestic market.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: That is when potentially we will mainly focus on drive.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So as you've heard me say before, 2.5, potentially 3 million people live within three hours radius of the Waikato region, including the region itself.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we'll definitely be focusing on the drive market.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: National events will be returning as well as national meetings and then eventually corporate travel, which again,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: business are quite a good lead for us with regards to the sector again and then lastly we're planning on one to two years before we actually start to see any sort of international impact again.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Short haul markets into Australia and the Pacific will open first and then we will eventually move back into major events, international conferences and starting to bring back the corporate and leisure travel market into Waikato.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: That's the timeframe we are currently doing our scenario planning for, but as we all know it may change every week based on the alert levels we have.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And then lastly, reimagine.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So you would have seen that last week or actually two weeks ago as well, the government announced that there was a call to reshape and reimagine the tourism industry.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we have been pushing for the last two to three months with Tourism New Zealand and the Minister for Tourism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: around what we're calling a reimagining of tourism.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Now is our chance that this is more than a shock, it's actually a bit of a circuit breaker for the sector and for all business sectors for us all to look and see how could we do our businesses differently.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We all knew that tourism was growing at quite an exponential rate and there were some areas that were really feeling the pressure such as Queenstown and Central Otago.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: and Cathedral Cove, some of our sites in Mount Tongarero.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So for us now, it's great to see the government are taking this seriously.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So we're now working with tourism New Zealand to lead a new plan domestically and internationally, but also look at all these types of questions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: You know, we're our previous visitor arrivals and expenditure growth.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: right for the region, what will success look like for us for the future for a visitor market?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: How do we move our sector from boom and bust again, no different to the food and fibre sector or any other business cycle?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And lastly, how do we move to more sustainable community-led tourism?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And for us in the Waikato, what we call creating net benefit, rather than just benefiting those that potentially were making money out of tourism, we want to see communities thrive.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And that they have the decision around what sort of tourism they want.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, we will be moving our 2016 tourism opportunities plan, which many of you were involved in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We will now be developing a destination management plan and that will be happening nationally as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: to try on what we call transform tourism for the better.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So there is no such word as recovery now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We will not be receiving the same sorts of level of tourism as we did previously, but neither we might not have the same sort of services and opportunities available in tourism as well post COVID.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: So again, it is a good opportunity for us to look at transforming the sector.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I think Sam, I think we've got one more.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: Looking lastly, just obviously a call, I call it to all my local government partners.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: I really appreciate everyone helping to promote your rates relief for missions and installment policies, not just for the tourism sector, but for all business.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: It's great to see you're all continuing with your resource consent process, maybe in a more delayed way, but again, the liquor and food licensing is gonna be more important for us, particularly when we try and stand up after alert level three.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: We still have the regional major event strategy activation, so again we are still awaiting a decision from Well Energy Trust about potentially helping to establish a regional major events fund which will actually assist all the districts to be able to bid for major events again and also to attract and bid new major events into our shoulder season across the region.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And then lastly, again, just to thank you all for your continual funding to support the tourism industry.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And that would obviously be more important through restart and the reimagined phase.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

segments.text: And again, the point I made earlier, we will not be recovering to pre COVID levels for at least two to five years as a sector.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_05

ocr_lines.text: call to reshape and reimagine the tourism industry for the future
Dlrectly engaged wlth Tourism NZ, Reglona
ourism NZ, Alr NZ and MBIE

intelligence.summary: The meeting covered various topics including apologies and attendance, tourism updates, and strategic plans for recovery post-COVID-19. Key discussions involved the impact of COVID-19 on tourism, government lobbying for extended wage subsidies, and the launch of the 'Mighty Local' campaign. A three-tiered recovery plan was outlined, focusing on mitigating impacts, restarting activities, and reimagining the tourism sector. Collaboration with local governments and Tourism New Zealand was emphasized for sustainable tourism development.

evidence.quote: Hazel is absent; Jim will leave at 1:30 for another meeting.

evidence.quote: Jason provides an update on tourism, highlighting significant growth in Waipa District pre-COVID and the impact of COVID on tourism.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_05
evidence.quote: Sharp decline in international visitors; baseline airline services maintained for repatriation and cargo.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_05
evidence.quote: Efforts to extend wage subsidies beyond 12 weeks due to prolonged recovery needs.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_05
evidence.quote: The 'Mighty Local' campaign was launched two weeks ago, focusing on the restart phase.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_05
evidence.quote: Three-tiered response plan: mitigate, restart, reimagine.

evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_05
evidence.quote: Government's call to reshape the tourism industry.
